page: Vultr voc-s-4c-32gb-640s-amd is an x86_64 architecture server within the Optimized Cloud Compute family. It features 4 vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor running at 2.0 GHz with a shared CPU allocation. The system is equipped with 32.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Local storage consists of a bundled 640 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU hardware is present on this configuration.

Benchmark data indicates a divergence between single-core and multi-core performance. Single-core CPU performance via stress-ng is positioned in the top tier, whereas multi-core performance and memory bandwidth fall into the average tier. Multi-threaded bzip3 decompression and static web serving throughput are weaker, placing in the bottom 25%. Database operations and medium-model LLM inference also show lower-tier performance, reflecting the constraints of the shared CPU allocation under sustained parallel workloads.

This server offers a high resource density of memory and NVMe storage relative to its shared CPU compute profile, making it a cost-efficient option for workloads requiring large datasets but moderate CPU utilization. It is best suited for memory-intensive applications, caching layers such as Redis, moderate database hosting, and general-purpose workloads that benefit from 32 GB of RAM and fast local storage without requiring dedicated, sustained multi-core CPU performance.
description: Vultr voc-s-4c-32gb-640s-amd is an x86_64 server in the Optimized Cloud Compute family, featuring 4 shared AMD EPYC-Rome vCPUs clocked at 2.0 GHz, 32.0 GB of RAM, and a 640 GB NVMe SSD. While its single-core CPU performance ranks in the top tier, its multi-core capabilities, memory bandwidth, and database operations perform in the average to weak ranges due to its shared CPU allocation. This resource profile provides a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core alongside substantial local NVMe storage, offering qualitative cost efficiency for memory-heavy tasks that do not demand dedicated CPU resources. It is well-suited for caching layers, memory-intensive applications, and moderate database workloads.
